and **Uḍi Śāhis**, were a dynasty established between 843 CE and 1026 CE. Enduring multiple waves of conquests for nearly two centuries, the western frontiers of their core territory comprised the regions of [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara") and [Kabulistan](/wiki/Kabulistan "Kabulistan") in contemporary [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") and [eastern](/wiki/Provinces_of_Afghanistan "Provinces of Afghanistan") [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), while eastern frontiers expanded to the [Sutlej](/wiki/Sutlej "Sutlej") River, encompassing the regions of [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab "Punjab") and the [Kangra Valley](/wiki/Kangra_Valley "Kangra Valley") in contemporary [Punjab, Pakistan](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan"), [Punjab, India](/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India"), and [Himachal Pradesh](/wiki/Himachal_Pradesh "Himachal Pradesh"). The empire was founded by Kallar in c. 843 CE after overthrowing Lagaturman, the last [Turk Shahi](/wiki/Turk_Shahis "Turk Shahis") king.

The **Hindu Shahis**, also referred to as the **Kabul Shahis** and **Uḍi Śāhis**, were a dynasty established between 843 CE and 1026 CE. Enduring multiple waves of conquests for nearly two centuries, the western frontiers of their core territory comprised the regions of [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara") and [Kabulistan](/wiki/Kabulistan "Kabulistan") in contemporary [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") and [eastern](/wiki/Provinces_of_Afghanistan "Provinces of Afghanistan") [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), while eastern frontiers expanded to the [Sutlej](/wiki/Sutlej "Sutlej") River, encompassing the regions of [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab "Punjab") and the [Kangra Valley](/wiki/Kangra_Valley "Kangra Valley") in contemporary [Punjab, Pakistan](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan"), [Punjab, India](/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India"), and [Himachal Pradesh](/wiki/Himachal_Pradesh "Himachal Pradesh"). The empire was founded by Kallar in c. 843 CE after overthrowing Lagaturman, the last [Turk Shahi](/wiki/Turk_Shahis "Turk Shahis") king.

The history of the Hindu Shahis begins in 843 CE with Kallar deposing the last [Turk Shahi](/wiki/Turk_Shahis "Turk Shahis") ruler, Lagaturman. Samanta succeeded him, and it was during his reign that the region of [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul") was lost to the [Persianate](/wiki/Persianate "Persianate") [Saffarid dynasty](/wiki/Saffarid_dynasty "Saffarid dynasty").[[6]](#cite_note-:1-8) Lalliya replaced Samanta soon after and re-conquered Kabul whilst also subduing the region of [Zabulistan](/wiki/Zabulistan "Zabulistan").[[7]](#cite_note-:2-9)[[8]](#cite_note-:3-10) He is additionally noteworthy for coming into conflict with [Samkaravarman](/wiki/Sankaravarman "Sankaravarman") of the [Utpala dynasty](/wiki/Utpala_dynasty "Utpala dynasty"), resulting in his victory and the latter's death in [Hazara](/wiki/Hazara_region "Hazara region").[[9]](#cite_note-:4-11) Bhimadeva, the next notable ruler, is most significant for vanquishing the [Samanid Empire](/wiki/Samanid_Empire "Samanid Empire") in Ghazni and Kabul in response to their conquests.[[10]](#cite_note-:5-12) Jayapala then gained control and was brought into conflict with the newly formed [Ghaznavid Empire](/wiki/Ghaznavid_Empire "Ghaznavid Empire"), however, he was eventually defeated. During his rule and that of his son and successor, Anandapala, the kingdom of [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore") was conquered. The following Shahi rulers all resisted the Ghaznavids but were ultimately unsuccessful, resulting in the downfall of the empire in 1026 CE.

## Sources

### Literature

No literature survives from Hindu Shahi courts. Unlike the case of Turk Shahis, only fragmented information can be obtained from chronicles of neighboring powers — Kashmir and [Ghaznavi](/wiki/Ghaznavids "Ghaznavids").[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2017-13)[[c]](#cite_note-14) Of the former, Kalhana's [Rajatarangini](/wiki/Rajatarangini "Rajatarangini") (1148-1149) is the only extant source.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2017-13) Of the latter, there are Tārīkh al-Hind by [Al-Biruni](/wiki/Al-Biruni "Al-Biruni") (c. 1030), [Tārīkh-i Bayhaqī](/wiki/Tarikh-i_Bayhaqi "Tarikh-i Bayhaqi") by Abu'l-Faḍl Bayhaqi (c. late 11th century),[[d]](#cite_note-16) Zayn al-Akhbar by [Abu Sa'id Gardezi](/wiki/Abu_Sa%27id_Gardezi "Abu Sa'id Gardezi"), and [Kitab-i Yamini](/wiki/Tarikh_Yamini "Tarikh Yamini") by al-Utbi (c. 1020).[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2017-13)[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197689-17)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976-15)

### Coins

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Coins_of_the_Shahis_8th_century.jpg/330px-Coins_of_the_Shahis_8th_century.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coins_of_the_Shahis_8th_century.jpg)

Some of the earliest coinage of the Hindu Shahis. *Obverse*: Recumbent bull, with [Nagari](/wiki/Nagari_script "Nagari script") legend:  
[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Nagari1035_shrii.jpg/20px-Nagari1035_shrii.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_shrii.jpg)[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Nagari1035_spa.jpg/20px-Nagari1035_spa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_spa.jpg)[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Nagari1035_l.svg/20px-Nagari1035_l.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_l.svg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Nagari1035_p.svg/20px-Nagari1035_p.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_p.svg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Nagari1035_ti.jpg/20px-Nagari1035_ti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_ti.jpg) *Sri Spalapati*  
*Reverse*: horserider with corrupted [Bactrian script](/wiki/Bactrian_script "Bactrian script"):  
**ςρι ςπaλaπaτι** *Sri Spalapati*  
i.e. "Lord Commander-in-chief".[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllchin2019458–459-18)

The Hindu Shahis issued silver [jital coinage](/wiki/Jital_coin "Jital coin") which underwent wide circulation from nearby [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh")[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlood201838-19) to northeastern Europe.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlood201825-20) They were first "discovered" by [James Tod](/wiki/James_Tod "James Tod"), a British orientalist in 1822.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976190-21) These coins exhibit progressive debasement with time, with a regular decrease of silver content, allowing for the sequencing of the coinage.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMACDOWALL1968-22) Early issues do not mention personal names but only generic titles, thereby not matching with the names found from literature.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan201723-23) The characteristic motif of a horseman on the reverse with a bull on the obverse goes back to the [Indo-Scythian](/wiki/Indo-Scythian "Indo-Scythian") ruler [Azes I](/wiki/Azes_I "Azes I").

### Inscriptions and archaeology

A. R. Rahman of the [Quaid-i-Azam University](/wiki/Quaid-i-Azam_University "Quaid-i-Azam University") and [Ahmad Hasan Dani](/wiki/Ahmad_Hasan_Dani "Ahmad Hasan Dani") did rudimentary field surveys in the late 1960s.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2017-13) Afterwards, the Italian Archaeological Mission in Pakistan (IAMP) extensively surveyed the regions in and around [Swat](/wiki/Swat_District "Swat District").[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2017-13) In 1996, Khan and Meister obtained a license from Department of Archaeology for an "integrated study of Hindu-Śāhi sites"; excavation at [Kafir-kot](/wiki/Kafir_Kot "Kafir Kot") and field-surveys of the [Salt Range](/wiki/Salt_Range "Salt Range") were engaged in with aid from the [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania") and the American Institute of Pakistan Studies.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeister20109-24)

Inscriptions remain scarce.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976218-25) Mostly found in [Udabhanda](/wiki/Udabhanda "Udabhanda"), they either commemorate the commissioning of temples or are affixed at the base of idol-pedestals.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976218-25) Of the former kind—[Mir Ali](/wiki/Mir_Ali,_Pakistan "Mir Ali, Pakistan") Inscription, [Dewal](/wiki/Dewal_Manal "Dewal Manal") Inscription, Dewai Inscription, Ratnamanjari Inscription, Veka Inscription, Hund Stone Inscription, Kamesvaridevi Inscription, Barikot Inscription, and Isvara Inscription—most are disfigured to various extents due to their use as grinding stones in medieval times and are decipherable only in parts.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976218-25)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhaw2016-26) The latter kind is relatively abundant but only provides snippets of trivia.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976218-25) The language is exclusively [Sharda](/wiki/Sharada_script "Sharada script").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976219-27)[[e]](#cite_note-28) A *samvat* is mentioned in all of them whose zero year is understood to correspond to 822 C.E. based on the Zalamkot Bilingual Inscription; it has been assumed to be initiated by Kallar on his coronation, as was typically the case for most Hindu dynasties of medieval India.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976241,_318-29)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERahman1998473-30) Copper land grants etc. are yet to be documented.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976218-25)

## Origins

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Horseman_on_Hindu_Shahi_coinage.jpg/250px-Horseman_on_Hindu_Shahi_coinage.jpg)](/wiki/File:Horseman_on_Hindu_Shahi_coinage.jpg)

Horseman on a coin of Spalapati, i.e. the "War-lord". The headgear has been interpreted as a [turban](/wiki/Turban "Turban").[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976187_and_Pl._V_B."the_horseman_is_shown_wearing_a_turban-like_head-gear_with_a_small_globule_on_the_top"-31)

[Al-Biruni](/wiki/Al-Biruni "Al-Biruni"), an Iranian [polyglot](/wiki/Polyglot "Polyglot") and the only contemporary source on the dynasty, described that the Hindu Shahis as [Brahmins](/wiki/Brahmins "Brahmins").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197651-32) However this view goes against the opinions of authors from later centuries. [Kashmiri](/wiki/Kashmiris "Kashmiris") historian, [Kalhana](/wiki/Kalhana "Kalhana") in his 12th century work [Rajatarangini](/wiki/Rajatarangini "Rajatarangini"), described the Hindu Shahis of being of [Kshatriya](/wiki/Kshatriya "Kshatriya") origin.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197651-32) [André Wink](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Wink "André Wink") confirms Biruni's description of the Shahi dynasty as Brahmins.[[28]](#cite_note-:0-33)

The 10th century Arab historian, [Al-Masudi](/wiki/Al-Masudi "Al-Masudi") mentioned in his work, that in his time, the kings of [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara") were all called "[![Arabic Hahaj for Hindu Shahis](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Arabic_Hahaj_for_Hindu_Shahis.jpg/20px-Arabic_Hahaj_for_Hindu_Shahis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arabic_Hahaj_for_Hindu_Shahis.jpg "Arabic Hahaj for Hindu Shahis")" (which has been variously read *Hajaj*, *J.haj* or *Ch'hach*), Elliot transliterated the character to "Hahaj" and Cunningham had it equated to the [Janjua tribe/clan](/wiki/Janjua "Janjua"), who were held to be descendants of the [Rouran Khaganate](/wiki/Rouran_Khaganate "Rouran Khaganate").[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197648–50-34) Rahman doubts this theory and instead transliterates to "J.haj", an Arabicised form of *[Chhachh](/wiki/Chhachh "Chhachh")*, while the area of Gandhara itself was called "country of the *Rahbūt*" ([Rājpūt](/wiki/Rajput "Rajput"))[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197648-35) which is even today the name of the region around the Hindu Shahi capital of [Hund](/wiki/Hund_(village) "Hund (village)").[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197648–50-34) In the 10th century, this region was occupied by the tribes of the [Gakhars](/wiki/Gakhars "Gakhars") and [Khokhars](/wiki/Khokhars "Khokhars"), who formed a large part of the Hindu Shahi army according to the Persian historian [Firishta](/wiki/Firishta "Firishta").[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197648–50-34)

Abdul Rahman accepted folklore among current inhabitants of Hund about pre-Muslim kings of the region belonging to the Hodi tribe, and proposed an Odi origin for the Hindu Shahis belonging to [Oddiyana](/wiki/Oddiyana "Oddiyana"), a region in [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara"). He also pointed to the famous Senvarma inscription as evidence in support and suggested **Odi Shahis** or **Uḍi Śāhis** as an accurate name for the dynasty.[[31]](#cite_note-36)[[32]](#cite_note-37)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERahman2002a41-38)[[f]](#cite_note-40) [Michael W. Meister](/wiki/Michael_W._Meister "Michael W. Meister") found Rahman's arguments to be convincing.[[35]](#cite_note-41)

## History

### Kallar: The first Hindu Shahi

#### Establishment

The [Abbasids](/wiki/Abbasids "Abbasids") led by caliph [Al-Ma'mun](/wiki/Al-Ma%27mun "Al-Ma'mun") defeated the Kabul branch of the [Turk Shahis](/wiki/Turk_Shahi "Turk Shahi") in 815 CE who had invaded Khorasan.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197685-42)[[37]](#cite_note-MA20-43) Following this defeat, the Turk Shahis not only had to convert to Islam but also had to cede key cities and regions.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197685-86-44) Another campaign against the Gandhara branch seems to have followed soon, with the Caliphate reaching as far East as the [Indus river](/wiki/Indus_river "Indus river") and imposing a critical defeat.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197686-45) A hefty annual tribute was to be paid in return for sovereignty rights to both territories.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197686-45)[[g]](#cite_note-47)

The Turk Shahis ended up in a precarious state and in c. 843 CE, the last ruler Lagaturman was deposed by one of his ministers, a [Brahmin](/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin") vazir called Kallar.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197687-88-48)[[42]](#cite_note-SKSK-49) The sole description of events comes from Al-Biruni:[[h]](#cite_note-51) Lagatarman's unbecoming manners had led his subjects to lodge multiple complaints with Kallar, who having chanced upon a treasure trove, was rapidly purchasing his way to power.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976-15) Kallar imprisoned the King for corruption and became the acting regent before usurping the throne permanently.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197688-52) The new "Hindu Shahi" dynasty was thus established in [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197691-53) None apart from Al-Biruni mentions Kallar; nothing is known about his rule or territorial extent or even his regnal dates.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197690,_93-94-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Hindu_Shahis_Spalapatideva_type%2C_Kabul_mint.jpg/250px-Hindu_Shahis_Spalapatideva_type%2C_Kabul_mint.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hindu_Shahis_Spalapatideva_type,_Kabul_mint.jpg)

Non-debased Spalapati series (Kabul mint) — weighs between 3.1 and 3.5 g with a uniform content of 70% silver. ***Obverse***: Bull with [trisula](/wiki/Trisula "Trisula") mark on the hump, with [Nagari](/wiki/Nagari_script "Nagari script") legend: [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Nagari1035_shrii.jpg/20px-Nagari1035_shrii.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_shrii.jpg)[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Nagari1035_spa.jpg/20px-Nagari1035_spa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_spa.jpg)[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Nagari1035_l.svg/20px-Nagari1035_l.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_l.svg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Nagari1035_p.svg/20px-Nagari1035_p.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_p.svg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Nagari1035_ti.jpg/20px-Nagari1035_ti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_ti.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Nagari1035_de.jpg/20px-Nagari1035_de.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_de.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Nagari1035_v.svg/20px-Nagari1035_v.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_v.svg) *Śri Spalapatideva* "Lord Commander-in-Chief". ***Reverse***: Horseman with [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Nagari1035_a.svg/20px-Nagari1035_a.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_a.svg) *a* in Nagari to left and symbol to right.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllchin2019458–459-18)

Historians such as that of [Alexander Cunningham](/wiki/Alexander_Cunningham "Alexander Cunningham") suggest that coin series bearing the obverse title 'Spalapati' ('Warlord) were minted by Kallar.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976-15) According to [Edward Clive Bayley](/wiki/Edward_Clive_Bayley "Edward Clive Bayley")'s misreading of the corrupted remains of a Bactrian legend as Arabic numerals, he proposed that another series of coins bearing the legend 'Samanta' ('Feudatory') were also minted by Kallar.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976186-55) He argued the 'Spalapati' series to have been minted for circulation in Persian regions of his territory and the 'Samanta' series for [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit_language "Sanskrit language")-speaking regions.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976186-55) and it has been adduced that Kallar may have felt insecure about the legitimacy of his rule as long as the imprisoned [Turk Shahi](/wiki/Turk_Shahis "Turk Shahis") ruler Lagaturman was alive, and hence affirmed his claim to leadership by such indirect titles.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197692-56)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Hindu_Shahis_coinage_in_the_name_of_Samanta_Deva_Kabul_mint.jpg/250px-Hindu_Shahis_coinage_in_the_name_of_Samanta_Deva_Kabul_mint.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hindu_Shahis_coinage_in_the_name_of_Samanta_Deva_Kabul_mint.jpg)

Rather debased Samantadeva series (Kabul mint) — weighs between 2.9 and 3.9 g with a variable content of 60 to 70% silver. ***Obverse***: Bull with [trisula](/wiki/Trisula "Trisula") mark on the hump, with [Nagari](/wiki/Nagari_script "Nagari script") legend: [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Nagari1035_shrii.jpg/20px-Nagari1035_shrii.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_shrii.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Nagari1035_s.svg/20px-Nagari1035_s.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_s.svg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Nagari1035_m.svg/20px-Nagari1035_m.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_m.svg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Nagari1035_t.svg/20px-Nagari1035_t.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_t.svg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Nagari1035_de.jpg/20px-Nagari1035_de.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_de.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Nagari1035_v.svg/20px-Nagari1035_v.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_v.svg) *[Śri](/wiki/Sri "Sri") Samantadeva*. ***Reverse***: Horseman with [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Nagari1035_bhii.jpg/20px-Nagari1035_bhii.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_bhii.jpg) *bhī* in [Nagari](/wiki/Nagari_script "Nagari script") to left and symbol to right.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllchin2019458–459-18)

The 'Spalapati' series may also have been minted by the last Turk Shahi rulers instead as 'Pati Dumi', who was defeated by the [Abbasid](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate") Caliph [Al-Ma'mun](/wiki/Al-Ma%27mun "Al-Ma'mun"), is described by [Al-Azraqi](/wiki/Al-Azraqi "Al-Azraqi") and [Al-Biruni](/wiki/Al-Biruni "Al-Biruni") as an 'Ispahbadh' ('Warlord'), equivalent to the title Spalapati.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197693,_175-57) Rahman therefore believes that Kallar did not initiate any changes in the currency system of the last Turk Shahis[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197696-58) and the Samanta series was minted by succeeding Hindu Shahi rulers.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197693-59) Numismatist and historian [Michael Alram](/wiki/Michael_Alram "Michael Alram")'s publications take note of this view;[[52]](#cite_note-60) however some scholars attribute the entirety of the bull/horserider coinage, including the Spalapati series, to the Hindu Shahis.[[53]](#cite_note-AM21-61)

### Samanta

Al-Biruni notes that Samanta was the successor of Kallar and may have been his son, but their genealogical relationship is left undescribed.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197695-62) Like in the case of Kallar, there is a total lack of information on his rule or even his actual name and he seems to have replicated the [Turk Shahi](/wiki/Turk_Shahis "Turk Shahis") system of producing no name on their coinage.[[55]](#cite_note-63) The Samanta series prototype was followed by all future Hindu Shahi rulers and even the Muslim Ghaznavids, who succeeded the Hindu Shahis.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiunta2006238-64)

#### Loss of Kabul

Main article: [Hindu Shahi-Saffarid wars](/wiki/Hindu_Shahi-Saffarid_wars "Hindu Shahi-Saffarid wars")

In 870 CE, [Ya'qub ibn al-Layth](/wiki/Ya%27qub_ibn_al-Layth_al-Saffar "Ya'qub ibn al-Layth al-Saffar"), the founder of the recently formed [Saffarid dynasty](/wiki/Saffarid_dynasty "Saffarid dynasty") marched onto [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul"). According to the [Tarikh-i Sistan](/wiki/Tarikh-i_Sistan "Tarikh-i Sistan"), the [Saffarids](/wiki/Saffarid_dynasty "Saffarid dynasty") had come into conflict with the [zunbil dynasty](/wiki/Zunbils "Zunbils") based in modern day [Ghazni](/wiki/Ghazni "Ghazni") and after having defeated them, a son of the Zunbils had fled into the area corresponding to [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul") resulting in Yaqub's invasion.[[6]](#cite_note-:1-8) It has been attested to by numerous sources that [Ya'qub](/wiki/Ya%27qub_ibn_al-Layth_al-Saffar "Ya'qub ibn al-Layth al-Saffar") had brought forth idols and elephants to the [Abbasid](/wiki/Abbasids "Abbasids") Caliph [Al-Mu'tamid](/wiki/Al-Mu%27tamid "Al-Mu'tamid") from Kabul however it not clear whether this is indicative of the city or of the [Kabul valley](/wiki/Kabul_Valley "Kabul Valley"), though according to Rehman the latter was most probable.[[57]](#cite_note-65) The [Rawżat aṣ-ṣafāʾ](/wiki/Raw%C5%BCat_a%E1%B9%A3-%E1%B9%A3af%C4%81%CA%BE "Rawżat aṣ-ṣafāʾ") states that the ruler of Kabul was made prisoner though it is not clear whether this was Samanta.[[58]](#cite_note-66) The region was in Saffarid control until 878 CE before being recaptured by Lalliya, the successor to Samanta.[[59]](#cite_note-67)

Saffarid coinage in Kabul, with Arabic

Abbasid [dirham](/wiki/Dirham "Dirham") weight standard. *Obverse*: Recumbent bull with [Nagari](/wiki/Nagari_script "Nagari script") legend [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Nagari1035_shrii.jpg/20px-Nagari1035_shrii.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_shrii.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Nagari1035_khuu.jpg/20px-Nagari1035_khuu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_khuu.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Nagari1035_d.svg/20px-Nagari1035_d.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_d.svg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Nagari1035_r.svg/20px-Nagari1035_r.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_r.svg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Nagari1035_y.svg/20px-Nagari1035_y.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_y.svg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Nagari1035_kah.svg/20px-Nagari1035_kah.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_kah.svg) (*Śrī Khūdarayakah*, "The fortunate small [Raja](/wiki/Raja "Raja")"), [trisula](/wiki/Trisula "Trisula") mark on the hump of the bull. *Reverse*: horseman with [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Nagari1035_m.svg/20px-Nagari1035_m.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_m.svg) (*ma*) in [Nagari](/wiki/Nagari_script "Nagari script") to left, **[عدل](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D8%B9%D8%AF%D9%84 "wikt:عدل")** (*’adl*, "[Justice](/wiki/Justice "Justice")") in Arabic to right.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlood201825-26-68)

#### Khudarayaka: Governor of the Kabul Valley

It is unknown what arrangements Ya'qub made for the governance of Kabul after his victory and imprisonment of the then ruler; we only have [Tarikh-i Sistan](/wiki/Tarikh-i_Sistan "Tarikh-i Sistan") noting that Kabul was under an unnamed Ya'qub governor as late as 878/879.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976105-69) It is speculated that this governor was some blood relation of Samanta who was favourably inclined to Islam and went on to take the title of Khudarayaka (Small King) as ascertainable from a series of coins.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976105-106-70)[[i]](#cite_note-72) As has been the case with previous rulers, there is a lack of information including about his actual name, course of rule and eventual fate.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976106-71) The unavailability of his coins in or around Gandhara points to his lack of control over the region, which did not come into contact with Ya'qub's expeditions and were likely held by Samanta's relatives.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976106-71)

### Lalliya

#### Re-conquest of Kabul and invasion of Ghazna

Lalliya was the first Shahi noted by [Kalhana](/wiki/Kalhana "Kalhana"), he is depicted as a great ruler with enormous strength to the standard where kings of other regions would seek shelter in his capital of [Udabhanda](/wiki/Udabhanda "Udabhanda"), a change from the previous capital of [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul").[[64]](#cite_note-73) [Ya'qub](/wiki/Ya%27qub_ibn_al-Layth_al-Saffar "Ya'qub ibn al-Layth al-Saffar") is not known to have annexed or invaded the country of [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara") and it is assumed by Rehman that it was under the kingship of Lalliya.[[65]](#cite_note-74)

Khudrayaka, the Saffarid governor of Kabul, is noted to have ended his reign in 880 CE, however it is unknown what brought about his downfall. It is assumed Lalliya was implicated as when [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul") is next mentioned in 900 CE, it is described as reverting back as Shahi territory.[[7]](#cite_note-:2-9) [Amr ibn al-Layth](/wiki/Amr_ibn_al-Layth "Amr ibn al-Layth") succeeded Yaqub as the Amr of the [Saffarid dynasty](/wiki/Saffarid_dynasty "Saffarid dynasty") in 879 CE. The [Tarikh-i Sistan](/wiki/Tarikh-i_Sistan "Tarikh-i Sistan") records 'two Indian kings', reconstructed as Toramana and Asata described as governors and sons of Lalliya, are stated to have taken advantage of [Amr al-Layth](/wiki/Amr_ibn_al-Layth "Amr ibn al-Layth")'s preoccupation with rebellions in Khorasan and to have successfully invaded [Ghazni](/wiki/Ghazni "Ghazni") in 900 CE, defeating the Saffarid governor named as Fardaghin, though the Tarikh does not make it clear whether it was the region of [Zabulistan](/wiki/Zabulistan "Zabulistan") or of the city.[[8]](#cite_note-:3-10)

#### Aggression with the Utpala dynasty

Kalhana notes that Lalliya was a significant ally of the [Gujrati](/wiki/Gujrat,_Pakistan "Gujrat, Pakistan") ruler Alakhana against the machinations of the [Utpala dynasty](/wiki/Utpala_dynasty "Utpala dynasty"), whose ruler [Samkaravarman](/wiki/Utpala_dynasty#Sankaravarman "Utpala dynasty") invaded the Hindu Shahis c. 902 CE, however Kalhana further states that Lalliya's 'mighty glory outshone the kings of the north'.[[66]](#cite_note-75) Samkaravarman was killed by a stray arrow in [Hazara](/wiki/Hazara_region "Hazara region"), with scholars stating Lalliya's role in his death.[[9]](#cite_note-:4-11) A year later, his successor [Gopalavarman](/wiki/Utpala_dynasty#Gopalavarman "Utpala dynasty") re-invaded Shahi territory to depose a rebellious Shahi, and installed Lalliya's son Toramana with the new name of "Kamaluka".[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976113-114,_117-76)[[j]](#cite_note-77)

> Rajat., v, 232-33: As superintendent of the treasury he (Prabhakaradeva, the prime minister of Gopalavarman - the successor of Samkaravarman ) plundered the riches of the amorous (queen) and vanquished the Sahi kingdom at Udabhanda. He bestowed the kingdom of the rebellious Sahi upon Toramana, Lilliya's son, and gave him the (new) name Kamaluka.

— [Kalhana](/wiki/Kalhana "Kalhana")

### Kamaluka

Nothing definite is known about the reign of the Hindu Shahi ruler Kamaluka, except that he was succeeded by his son, Bhimadeva. Concurrent to his reign, the Saffarids rapidly lost their power to the [Samanids](/wiki/Samanid_Empire "Samanid Empire")[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976117-78) and sometime after 913 CE, the power vacuum led to the rise of a friendly power in the Ghazna province, the [Lawik dynasty](/wiki/Lawik_dynasty "Lawik dynasty"), which flourished until 962 CE and engaged in marital ties with the Hindu Shahis.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976118-119-79) There are various unsubstantiated speculations regarding the end date of Kamaluka's reign, ranging from 900 to 950.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976120-80)[[k]](#cite_note-81)

### Bhimadeva

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Hindu_Shahi_coinage_Circa_950_CE_Horseman_right%2C_holding_banner%3B_na_in_Nagari_to_left%3B_trace_of_symbol_to_right._%C5%9Bri_bhima_deva_on_the_reverse.jpg/250px-Hindu_Shahi_coinage_Circa_950_CE_Horseman_right%2C_holding_banner%3B_na_in_Nagari_to_left%3B_trace_of_symbol_to_right._%C5%9Bri_bhima_deva_on_the_reverse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hindu_Shahi_coinage_Circa_950_CE_Horseman_right,_holding_banner;_na_in_Nagari_to_left;_trace_of_symbol_to_right._%C5%9Bri_bhima_deva_on_the_reverse.jpg)

Strongly debased Bhimadeva series — found near-exclusively in Afghanistan and weighs between 3.1 and 3.2 g. ***Obverse***: Recumbent bull with legend [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Nagari1035_shrii.jpg/20px-Nagari1035_shrii.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_shrii.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Nagari1035_bhii.jpg/20px-Nagari1035_bhii.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_bhii.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Nagari1035_m.svg/20px-Nagari1035_m.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_m.svg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Nagari1035_de.jpg/20px-Nagari1035_de.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_de.jpg) [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Nagari1035_v.svg/20px-Nagari1035_v.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_v.svg) *Śri Bhīmadeva*. ***Reverse***: Horseman holding banner; [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Nagari1035_n.svg/20px-Nagari1035_n.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nagari1035_n.svg) *na* in Nagari to left; trace of symbol to right.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllchin2019458–459-18)

Mentioned as 'Bhima' in [Al-Biruni](/wiki/Al-Biruni "Al-Biruni")'s list, and identified with the *Śri Bhīmadeva* coin series, Bhimadeva was one of the most accomplished rulers of the Hindu Shahis alongside Lalliya.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976121-82) His rise to power was concurrent with the growth of neighbouring Hindu kingdoms such as that of the [Pala Empire](/wiki/Pala_Empire "Pala Empire"). According to the Khajuraho stone inscription, the [Kangra Valley](/wiki/Kangra_Valley "Kangra Valley") was under the authority of a Shahi king assumed to be Bhimadeva, and it is further presumed that the city of Bhimanagar in present day [Kangra](/wiki/Kangra,_Himachal_Pradesh "Kangra, Himachal Pradesh") was named after him.[[72]](#cite_note-83) The [Rajatarangini](/wiki/Rajatarangini "Rajatarangini") states that Bhima's daughter was married to the [Lohara dynasty](/wiki/Lohara_dynasty "Lohara dynasty") king of Kashmir, and his grand-daughter noted as [Didda](/wiki/Didda "Didda") became the Queen and last ruler of the [Utpala dynasty](/wiki/Utpala_dynasty "Utpala dynasty").[[73]](#cite_note-84)

#### Victory over the Samanid Empire

In the final years of Bhima's reign in c. 962, [Alp-Tegin](/wiki/Alp-Tegin "Alp-Tegin"), a rebel Turkish chief of the [Samanid Empire](/wiki/Samanid_Empire "Samanid Empire"), had annexed the regions of [Zabulistan](/wiki/Zabulistan "Zabulistan") and [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul") with the aim of waging holy war against the [Lawik dynasty](/wiki/Lawik_dynasty "Lawik dynasty") and the Hindu Shahis. The Lawik king fled to the Shahi domain in hopes of gaining re-inforcements to conquer the lost territory and in c. 963 Bhimadeva was successful in capturing [Ghazni](/wiki/Ghazni "Ghazni"). This victory is engraved in the Hund Slab Inscription dated to c. 989 CE during the reign of the succeeding Shahi Jayapala.[[10]](#cite_note-:5-12)

> [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Hund_inscription_of_Jayapaladeva%2C_in_the_year_146_%2810th_century_CE%29.jpg/250px-Hund_inscription_of_Jayapaladeva%2C_in_the_year_146_%2810th_century_CE%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hund_inscription_of_Jayapaladeva,_in_the_year_146_(10th_century_CE).jpg)
>
> Hund inscription of Jayapaladeva.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976-15)
>
> ...To the north of the Indus, which is a mass of complete merit here on earth, there is (a city) by name [Udabhandra](/wiki/Udabhandra "Udabhandra"), which has been made their home by learned men forming communities, just as [Meru](/wiki/Mount_Meru "Mount Meru") (was made their home) by the immortal (gods) and other (supernatural beings)...
>
> ...Therein dwelt the chief of kings, Bhīma, of terrible valour (or with valour like that of [Bhīma](/wiki/Bhima "Bhima"), the legendary hero), by whom, having conquered his enemies' troops, the earth was protected...  
> ...The king of that (country) is (now) Jayapaladeva, who, through his body, origin, and birth, has become the sole hero, whose very pure fame, having left heaven, has attained the eternal abode of Brahman....

— Excerpts of the Hund inscription of Jayapala (HSI), inscribed Year 146 (968 CE). Translation by Abdur Rehman.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1978-85)

Bhima's death is chronologically placed within the span of 964 to 965 CE. The Hund Slab Inscription attributes his passing to him 'burning himself through Shivas desire but not through the terrible enemy', suggesting a ritualistic suicide, and the absence of any noted political setbacks further supports the inference that his death occurred under such circumstances. In c.965 CE [Ghazni](/wiki/Ghazni "Ghazni") was recaptured from the [Lawik dynasty](/wiki/Lawik_dynasty "Lawik dynasty") by [Abu Ishaq](/wiki/Abu_Ishaq_Ibrahim_of_Ghazna "Abu Ishaq Ibrahim of Ghazna"), the successor of Alp-Tegin, after Bhimas death.[[75]](#cite_note-86)

### Jayapala

See also: [First Battle of Laghman](/wiki/First_Battle_of_Laghman "First Battle of Laghman"), [Second Battle of Laghman](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Laghman "Second Battle of Laghman"), and [Battle of Peshawar (1001)](/wiki/Battle_of_Peshawar_(1001) "Battle of Peshawar (1001)")

Bhimadeva's successors would all have the surname of "Pala", and Muslim sources give hazy indications of a successional dispute, leading many to suggest that the same family was not ruling anymore.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976131-133-87) Rahman disagrees that there exist sufficient evidence in favour of such a hypothesis or conclusion.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976131-133-87)

One Vijayapaladeva (r. 942 or 963) is obtained from the Ratnamanjari Inscription where he is held to be the 'supreme sovereign' or '*[Maharajadhiraja](/wiki/Maharajadhiraja "Maharajadhiraja")'*.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhaw2016-26) Rahman proposed that Vijayapaladeva had to have either belonged to the Kabul branch or had been a local Shahi feudatory.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976-15) Khaw disagrees and instead equates Vijayapaladeva with one Thakkana Sahi, mentioned by Kalhana as a rebel who had to be captured by Queen [Didda](/wiki/Didda "Didda") of Kashmir.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhaw2016-26) For Khaw, this identification fits within the narrative of Muslim sources; Jayapala ascended only after this threat was neutralized.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhaw2016-26)[[l]](#cite_note-88)

#### Resisting the Ghaznavids

[Bilgetegin](/wiki/Bilgetegin "Bilgetegin") succeeded Abu Ishaq Ibrahim on the occasion of his death in November 966, and ruled for about nine years, before being assassinated during his invasion of [Gardiz](/wiki/Gardez "Gardez"), the last bastion of the Lawiks.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976133-89) His successor [Piri](/wiki/B%C3%B6ritigin_of_Ghazni "Böritigin of Ghazni") was described as a drunkard whose oppressive rule led the citizens of Ghazna to request the return of Lawik.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976133-89) Lawik mounted yet another expedition with help from the "son of Kabul Shah" and met the Muslim forces in the area of [Charkh](/wiki/Charkh_District "Charkh District").[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976133-134-90) Both breathed their last in the war and the Muslim forces imposed an overwhelming victory, despite their numerical inferiority.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976134-91) [Sabuktigin](/wiki/Sabuktigin "Sabuktigin") became the undisputed leader of the Ghazni region, as he would go on to overthrow Piri.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976134-135-92) Kabul was lost forever and the foundation stone of the [Ghaznavid Empire](/wiki/Ghaznavid_Empire "Ghaznavid Empire") was cast.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976134-135-92)

In 986–987, Jayapala marched towards Ghazni and met with [Sabuktigin](/wiki/Sabuktigin "Sabuktigin")'s forces at Ghuzak.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976135-93)[[m]](#cite_note-95) The war remained largely inconclusive for days before the tide turned against the Shahis: Jayapala was forced to propose a peace treaty.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976135-136-96)[[n]](#cite_note-100) [Mahmud](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghazni "Mahmud of Ghazni"), son of Sabuktigin and a battle commander, wished to inflict a decisive defeat, but had to concede when Jayapala threatened to incinerate all valuables.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976136-101)[[o]](#cite_note-102) A war indemnity of one million Shahi dirhams and fifty war elephants was agreed upon and some frontier forts were ceded to the Ghaznavids.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976136-101) Accordingly, Jaypala made his way back with Ghaznavid commanders who were to take charge of the ceded forts, while some of his relatives and officials were left with Sabuktigin as hostages.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976136-101) Once Jayapala reached his own territories, he called off the treaty and threw the commanders into prison, hoping to force Sabuktigin into exchanging hostages.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976136-101)

Sabuktigin refused to believe that the treaty had been breached, but once it was established beyond doubt, he plundered the frontier town of [Lamghan](/wiki/Lamghan "Lamghan"): temples were demolished and houses burnt down.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976136-137-103) In response, Jayapala secured troops from unidentified Rajas,[[p]](#cite_note-106) and met with the Ghaznavids near Kindi (modern day [Kandibagh](/wiki/Kandibagh "Kandibagh")?).[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197614,_137-107) The Ghaznavids breached the enemy lines repeatedly using light attacks and followed them with an all-out assault, routing the Shahis who had to flee beyond the Indus despite their overwhelming numerical superiority.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976137-138-108) The entire span of territory up to Peshawar was lost, and Sabuktigin installed his own tax-collectors; local tribes were ordained into Ghaznavid arms too.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976138-109) A [*ribāṭ*](/wiki/Ribat "Ribat") was commissioned at Kindi to commemorate the victory.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197614-110) However, Peshawar and adjacent regions returned to the Shahis sometime soon, probably during what would be a long interlude in the Ghaznavid-Shahi conflict.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976138-109)[[q]](#cite_note-111)

Circa 990–991, Mahmud would be imprisoned by his father Sabuktigin on grounds of fomenting a rebellion.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976138-109) Jayapala probably tried to leverage the rift in his favor by promising to rescue Mahmud, marry off his daughter to him, and further, allot sufficient wealth and troops.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976138-139-112) Mahmud did not respond favorably and noting the Shahi to be a non-muslim, proclaimed his absolute devotion to Sabuktigin and pledged to attack Jayapala upon release.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976139-113)

#### Annexation of Lahore

Around the same time, Jayapala was challenged by Bharat, a Raja of [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore") who wished to wrest control of [Nandana](/wiki/Nandana "Nandana"), Jailam and Takeshar.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976139-113) Anandapala, then Governor of Punjab and son of Jayapala, was ordered to intercept Bharat's forces and in the ensuing battle, Bharat was imprisoned and Lahore annexed; however the nobility of Lahore pleaded on behalf of their old King, who was reinstated as a feudatory after payment of tributes.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976139-140-114) About a year hence, Bharat's son Chandrak deposed him on the grounds of waging an ill-thought-out campaign against the Shahis, and became the new feudatory.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976140-115) For reasons which are not clear, c. 998-999 (eight years after the usurpation), Jayapala declared war against Lahore on the pretext of protecting his suzerain Bharat and dispatched Anandapala.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976140-115) Chandrak was ambushed and kidnapped around the battleground of Samutla, and Lahore was annexed by the Shahis.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976140-141-116) Rahman speculates that the Shahis were trying to balance their losses to the Ghaznavids using any pretext.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976140-115)

#### Death

In 998 CE, Mahmud ascended the Ghaznavid throne at Ghazni, and went on an annexation spree.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976141-117) Soon, Mahmud turned his eyes on the Shahis, allegedly resolving to invade their territories every year.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976141-117) In what was the last battle of his life, Jayapala met with Mahmud in the [Battle of Peshawar](/wiki/Battle_of_Peshawar_(1001) "Battle of Peshawar (1001)") on 27 September 1001; one Shahi governor of the Bardari province named Adira Afghan is held to have switched sides and aided in the safe and quick passage of Mahmud's troops across Shahi provinces.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976141-142-118) Mahmud saw through Jayapala's tactics of delaying the conflict in the hope of receiving reinforcements and declared war immediately.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976142-119) Soon, the Shahis were in a state of disarray with Jayapala and fifteen of his relatives taken as prisoners.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976142-119) About one million Shahi forces were taken as slaves.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976143-120) The war-spoils awed contemporary chroniclers: the royal necklaces alone were valued at over six million Shahi dirhams.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976142-143-121) Mahmud continued his raid as far as [Hund](/wiki/Hund_(village) "Hund (village)"), as his forces chased fleeing troops and decimated pockets of resistance.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976143-120) Within a few months, the entire Shahi territory to the west of the Indus had submitted to Mahmud.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976143-120)[[r]](#cite_note-123)

Jayapala was eventually released but Muslim chroniclers differ about the specifics.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976143-120) [Unsuri](/wiki/Unsuri "Unsuri"), a court-poet of Mahmud notes that he was sold in the slave market; [Minhaj ad-din](/wiki/Minhaj-i_Siraj_Juzjani "Minhaj-i Siraj Juzjani") and al-Malik Isami adds a price of 80 dirhams.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976144-145-124) Others like al-Ansab note that Mahmud had rejected his request for pardon but allowed him to be free in lieu of a payment of 2.5 million dirhams and 50 war-elephants around March 1002, which Rahman finds more likely.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976146-125) Jayapala returned to Hund and immolated himself in a pyre after abdicating the throne in favor of Anandapala.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976146-147-126)

The Hindu Shahis and main South Asian polities in 1000, on the eve of the [Ghaznavid](/wiki/Ghaznavid_Empire "Ghaznavid Empire") invasions of the subcontinent.[[109]](#cite_note-127)[[110]](#cite_note-128)

Anandapala ascended to the throne around April 1002.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976147-129) His capital city remains unknown but was likely Nandan.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976147-148-130)[[s]](#cite_note-131) Anandapala had entered into marital relations with Tunga, the prime-minister of [Didda](/wiki/Didda "Didda"), then-ruler of Kashmir and had at least two sons.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976157-132) He commanded significant fame as a patron of scholars though texts from his court are not extant.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976157-132)

Circa April 1006, Mahmud requested Anandapala to consent to the passage of his troops via his territories to reach [Daud](/wiki/Fateh_Daud "Fateh Daud"), the ruler of Multan.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976148-133) He declined the request and even went to the extent of stationing troops on the banks of Indus to prevent Mahmud's crossing, an enraged Mahmud waged a cataclysmic war upon the Shahis and compelled Anandapala to escape to Kashmir before eventually finishing his original objective of conquering Multan.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976148-149-134) All these territories of "Hind" were left under the governorship of a certain Sukhapala, a neo-convert.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976149-150-135)

However, a couple of years hence, Sukhapala renounced Islam (c. late 1006) and declared rebellion.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976149-150-135) At this juncture, Anandapala tried to make space for himself by promising to aid Mahmud in containing Turk rebellions at the other side of his empire; apparently, he did not want a ruler who had defeated him, to be defeated by another.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976150-136)[[t]](#cite_note-137) It is unknown whether Anandapala's offer was accepted but Mahmud stalled his chase of Ilaq Khan and turned his attention to the Shahis; Sukhapala offered negligible resistance before fleeing into Kashmur from where he was captured, fined, and imprisoned to death.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976150-136) It is likely that Anandapala was installed as the next Ghaznavid vassal.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976-15)

C. December 1008, Mahmud mounted an invasion of Hindu Shahis for reasons which are not clear.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976151-138) Anandapala sent a large army, supplanted with neighboring troops under the commandership of his son, Trilochanapala, who arrived in the plains of [Chach](/wiki/Chhachh "Chhachh") but failed to prevent Mahmud's troops from crossing across the Indus.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976151-138) The [Battle of Chach](/wiki/Battle_of_Chach "Battle of Chach") ended with the defeat of the Hindu Shahis.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976151-152-139) Mahmud chased the fugitive troops for months, seizing [Nagarkot](/wiki/Nagarkot,_India "Nagarkot, India") to collect his war-spoils, in the process and even took a son of Anandapala as hostage.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976152-153-140) Governors were installed across the conquered provinces and Mahmud would return to Ghazni.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976-15)

This would be the last military conflict of Anandapala; the next year, Anandapala sent an embassy to Mahmud.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976155-141) The proposal of peace was accepted and in return, Hindu Shahis were to accept tributary status, provide (limited) military support, guarantee passage of troops, and remit an annual tribute.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976155-141) Mahmud sent his own agents to oversee the enforcement of the peace treaty and within a year, normal trade relations had resumed.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976155-141) The death of Anandapala is not recorded in any chronicle; however, it can be ascertained to be c. late 1010 - early 1011.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976156-142) The fate of the son taken back to Ghazni remains unknown.

### Trilochanapala and Bhimapala

Al-Biruni held that Trilochanapala had a favorable attitude towards Muslim subjects, unlike his father.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976157-132) Trilochanapala did not dishonor Anandapala's treaty, but when Mahmud wished to march towards [Thanesar](/wiki/Thanesar "Thanesar") via Hindu Shahi territories, he proposed that the city be spared in lieu of a negotiated peace treaty.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976157-158-143) Mahmud rejected the request and sacked Thanesar with an uneventful passage via Shahi territories.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976158-144) However, as a consequence or otherwise, Trilochanapala soon stopped paying the annual tributes to Mahmud and declared war.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976158-144)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Mahmud_of_Ghazni_on_an_elephant.jpg/250px-Mahmud_of_Ghazni_on_an_elephant.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mahmud_of_Ghazni_on_an_elephant.jpg)

[Mahmud of Ghazni](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghazni "Mahmud of Ghazni") riding an elephant following his conquest in India.

In November 1013, Mahmud progressed towards Hind to contain Trilochanapala but failed to make it across the snow-laden passes.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976159-145) Taking advantage of this delay, Trilochanapala tasked his son Bhimapala with arranging Shahi troops and went to Kashmir, where he received a battalion from king [Sangramaraja](/wiki/Sangramaraja "Sangramaraja") of the [Lohara dynasty](/wiki/Lohara_dynasty "Lohara dynasty"), commanded by Tunga.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976159-145) The face-off happened in the middle of the following year.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976-15) Bhimapala initially went about exploiting the local topography of a narrow mountain-pass in his favor, and launched stinging guerrilla attacks on Mahmud's troops—to the extent of being referred to by Uth'bi as "Bhima, the Fearless", until he got confident of his numerical superiority and switched to open-warfare; in the mayhem that followed this tactical blunder, the Shahis were routed and Bhima had to flee.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976159-160-146)

The fortress at [Nandana](/wiki/Nandana "Nandana") was sacked for war-spoils and a Ghaznavid governor was installed, while Mahmud went searching for Trilochanapala.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976161-147) Trilochanpala, in the meantime, had set up his base with Kashmiri forces on the banks of the [Poonch River](/wiki/Poonch_River "Poonch River").[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976162-148) An initial round of success against a Ghaznavid reconnaissance party contributed to Tunga's pride and he then mounted a disastrous maneuver without consulting experienced Shahi generals, ensuring another crippling defeat coupled with a total loss of territory, west of Tausi.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976162-148) Rahman noted this campaign to be the death-blow for the Hindu Shahis — "it was no longer a question of whether but a question of when" the Shahis would perish.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976162-148)

From the outset of his rule, Trilochanapala had chosen to expand into the [Siwalik Hills](/wiki/Siwalik_Hills "Siwalik Hills") to make up for the territories lost in his predecessors' conflicts with the Ghaznavids: this brought him into multiple conflicts with Chandar Rai of [Sharwa](/wiki/Sharwa "Sharwa").[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976162-148) But the fatal encounter with Mahmud ensured that Trilochanapala had nowhere but the Siwaliks to retreat into and compelled him to enter into a peace treaty, even offering his son to be married to the daughter of Chandar.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976162-163-149) The offer was accepted but Bhima was imprisoned when he went to bring the bride home and Chandar asked for reparations.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976163-150) This brought an end to Trilochanapala's imperial ambitions in the Lower Himalayas for the time being though stray conflicts continued.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976163-150)

When Mahmud sacked Sharwa while returning from his [Kanauj](/wiki/Kanauj "Kanauj") campaign (c. 1017), Trilochanapala is noted to have taken refuge with [Paramara Bhoja](/wiki/Bhoja "Bhoja").[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976163-164-151) Sometime soon, significant polities in the [Doab](/wiki/Doab "Doab") entered into treaties with one another and with the Hindu Shahis to ward off future invasions of a similar scale.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976164-152) Mahmud did not take kindly to these alliances and returned in October 1019.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976164-152) Trilochanapala's men were tasked by [Vidyadhara of Chandela](/wiki/Vidyadhara_(Chandela_ruler) "Vidyadhara (Chandela ruler)") to prevent Mahmud's troops from crossing across the [Ramganga](/wiki/Ramganga "Ramganga") (somewhere around [Bulandshahr](/wiki/Bulandshahr "Bulandshahr")) and they took positions at the eastern bank but failed to execute the task.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976164-152) Subsequently, Trilochanapala planned to move away, probably to join Vidyadhara's forces for the main faceoff, but a swift charge by Mahmud's troops inflicted yet another resounding defeat.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976165-153) Bulandshahr was sacked and two of his wives and daughters imprisoned.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976165-153) He tried to enter into a peace-treaty but in vain, causing him to flee to Vidyadhara.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976165-153) It is not known whether he made it to the camp but Vidyadhara is noted to have deserted his posts by then.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976166-154)

In 1021,[[u]](#cite_note-155) Trilochanapala, by then a ruler of little significance in all probabilities, was assassinated by his mutinous Hindu troops for reasons unknown.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976166-154) Bhimapala, who must have escaped the Rais sometime in between, succeeded him and continued to rule until 1026; nothing is known about his rule or territories.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976166-167-156)

### Unsuccessful renaissance

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/MassudOfGhazniCoin.jpg/250px-MassudOfGhazniCoin.jpg)](/wiki/File:MassudOfGhazniCoin.jpg)

[Ghaznavid bilingual coinage](/wiki/Ghaznavid_bilingual_coinage "Ghaznavid bilingual coinage") of [Mas'ud I of Ghazni](/wiki/Mas%27ud_I_of_Ghazni "Mas'ud I of Ghazni") (r. 1030-1040 CE), derived from Hindu Shahi designs, with the name of Mas'ud ([Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"): مسعود) around the head of the horserider.

*Adab al-harb*—a manual of state-craft produced during the times of [Iltutmish](/wiki/Iltutmish "Iltutmish"), which contains a host of unique information about the Ghaznavids—note that in 1040, one Sandbal, a grandson of the Kabul Shah, marched towards Lahore seeking to utilize the [imprisonment of Masʽud I](/wiki/Mas%CA%BDud_I_of_Ghazni#Death_and_aftermath "Masʽud I of Ghazni") and resulting political instability to his favor.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976329-330-157) The armies met at Qadar Jur (var. Qalachur) and despite the Shahis having numerical superiority, they were defeated as their troops left the battle in a state of panic once Sandbal was assassinated by a Turk archer.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976329-330-157)[[137]](#cite_note-158) He seemed to have been based around the Siwaliks and might have been a Shahi heir — many contemporary Muslim chronicles do mention a Hindu triumvirate to have unsuccessfully attacked the Ghaznavids around the same time but mention only two of the names, both petty Siwalik chieftains and not Sandbal.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976329-330-157) Some Shahis migrated into Kashmir and gained prominent positions in their court.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197690,_322-325-159)

## Culture and architecture

### Culture

[Shaivism](/wiki/Shaivism "Shaivism") was practised by the Hindu Shahis and likely was also the predominant religion; Saura was practised by some subjects, as were [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam").[[139]](#cite_note-160)[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197633-34-161)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhaw2016-26) Kabul exported cotton clothing and indigo.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman197630-162) [Ibn Hawqal](/wiki/Ibn_Hawqal "Ibn Hawqal") mentions the high quality cotton and wool industries during Shahi reign in which exports to China and Khorusan were noted. Silver ore was also smelted in [Andarab](/wiki/Andarab "Andarab") and mining occurring in the [Panjshir](/wiki/Panjshir_Province "Panjshir Province") region.[[142]](#cite_note-163)

It is also noted that the [Bactrian script](/wiki/Bactrian_script "Bactrian script") during the Hindu Shahis was discontinued and instead replaced with the [Sharada script](/wiki/Sharada_script "Sharada script"). The dress customs were noted as clothing consisting of cotton outer garments, trousers and shoes with men shaving their hair and beards.[[143]](#cite_note-164) A gold coin of Bhimadeva describes him as wearing a [Dhoti](/wiki/Dhoti "Dhoti") and [Uttariya](/wiki/Uttariya "Uttariya").[[144]](#cite_note-165)

### Architecture

New temples were built inside fortresses while existing ones were extensively refurbished or repurposed.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeister2010-166) The Gandhar-[Nagara](/wiki/Nagara_architecture "Nagara architecture") style of architecture developed distinct formulations under the Hindu Shahis.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeister2010-166) Meister notes a typical Hindu Shahi temple to have two ground-level chambers embedded within a tower leading to a *[minaret](/wiki/Minaret "Minaret")* like appearance with an [ambulatory](/wiki/Ambulatory "Ambulatory") at the top, that is accessible by a stairwell.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeister201012-167) He dates construction of eight temples to the Hindu Shahis, six of which are photographed below.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeister201036-38-168) There were also two sandstone temples at [Malot](/wiki/Malot,_Islamabad "Malot, Islamabad") and Shiv-Gangā (10th c.) which exhibited a blend of Shahi and Kashmiri architecture, bearing testimony to the cultural flows between the two polities.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeister201057-59-169)

The archaeology of the Hindu Shahis remains unrecognized and poorly understood.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeister20105-173) Dani ascribed ruined forts to the Hindu Shahis at [Pehur](/wiki/Pehure_Hamlet "Pehure Hamlet"), Kamala, and Bata, but without detailed reasoning.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976268-174) Hund remains the main archaeological site.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976269-175) Fragmentary evidence is located across the Peshawar valley.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeister20104-176) Excavations by Rahman et al, documented a Buddhist monastery at [Barikot](/wiki/Barikot "Barikot"), which was repurposed to a Hindu Shahi fort.

## Scholarship

Scholarship on Hindu Shahis[[x]](#cite_note-178) remains scarce.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2017-13) [Enmity towards the Hindu-majority India](/wiki/Anti-Indian_sentiment#Pakistan "Anti-Indian sentiment") (also see [Pakistan textbook controversy](/wiki/Pakistani_textbooks_controversy "Pakistani textbooks controversy")) & glorification of Mahmud Sebuktegin by the Pakistani government is considered to be main reasons behind lack of scholarship on pre-Islamic regional polities of the country.[[155]](#cite_note-179)

Colonial scholars—[James Prinsep](/wiki/James_Prinsep "James Prinsep"), [Alexander Cunningham](/wiki/Alexander_Cunningham "Alexander Cunningham"), [Henry Miers Elliot](/wiki/Henry_Miers_Elliot "Henry Miers Elliot"), [Edward Thomas](/wiki/Edward_Thomas_(antiquarian) "Edward Thomas (antiquarian)") et al.—had published on the Hindu Shahis, primarily from a numismatic perspective.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976xii-xiii-180) The first comprehensive volume on the subject appeared in 1972 by Yogendra Mishra, a professor in the Department of History of [Patna University](/wiki/Patna_University "Patna University"); he explored the Rajatarangini meticulously but lacked in numismatics and paleography.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976xiii-181) The next year, Deena Bandhu Pandey—Professor of Art History at [Banaras Hindu University](/wiki/Banaras_Hindu_University "Banaras Hindu University")—published his doctoral dissertation but his handling of Muslim sources, coins etc. were laden with errors, primarily stemming from an exclusive dependence upon English translations of Arabic/Persian chronicles.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976xiii-xiv-182) Both of these works are considered outdated and inaccurate, at large.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2017-13)

In 1979, Abdur Rehman received his [PhD](/wiki/PhD "PhD") from [Australian National University](/wiki/Australian_National_University "Australian National University") on "history, archaeology, coinage, and paleography" of the Turk Shahis and Hindu Shahis under the supervision of [Arthur Llewellyn Basham](/wiki/Arthur_Llewellyn_Basham "Arthur Llewellyn Basham").[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2017-13)[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976xvi-183) He has since published on the subject extensively and is considered to be an authority on the subject.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2017-13)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeister20109-24) In 2010, [Michael W. Meister](/wiki/Michael_W._Meister "Michael W. Meister")—Chair Professor of Art-History at UPenn—published a monograph on the temple-architecture of Sahis; he had worked with Rahman on multiple field investigations.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeister20109-24) In 2017, Ijaz Khan received his [PhD](/wiki/PhD "PhD") from the School of Ancient History and Archaeology of the [University of Leicester](/wiki/University_of_Leicester "University of Leicester") on "Settlement Archaeology of the Hindu Shahi[s] in North-Western Pakistan."[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhan2017-13)
